file_name: Wang - 2026 - Skin disease diagnosis using convolutional neural network techniques.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with transfer learning (DenseNet201 architecture) to perform multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. The study achieved a classification accuracy of 97% for diagnosing seven distinct skin disease categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a specific intelligent skin disease diagnosis framework using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with DenseNet201 architecture. It details a methodology involving the HAM10000 dataset, data preprocessing, augmentation, and model training, and reports new empirical results, specifically achieving 97% classification accuracy. Although it includes a literature survey, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new technical system rather than a summary of prior work.

title: SKIN DISEASE DIAGNOSIS USING CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK TECHNIQUES
journal: International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology and Science
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated skin disease diagnosis framework using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with DenseNet201 transfer learning to classify dermoscopic images into seven distinct disease categories. The system was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, employing preprocessing, data augmentation, and RandomOverSampler to address class imbalance. The model achieved a classification accuracy of 97% after 20 epochs of training. Beyond prediction, the system provides detailed medical information about the detected disease and recommends nearby healthcare facilities. The results demonstrate the potential of deep learning to support dermatologists in early detection and remote healthcare accessibility.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin disease categories (Melanoma, Actinic keratoses, Vascular lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic nevi)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images using a CNN with DenseNet201 transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for seven disease classes, achieving 97% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A collection of dermatoscopic images used for research in skin disease diagnosis, containing both benign and malignant skin lesions. It was uploaded into the application using pandas.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model with DenseNet201 architecture after data preprocessing, augmentation, and RandomOverSampler application. 80% of the dataset was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the model. 20% of the dataset was used for testing.
use.other: Images from this dataset (or any type of skin image) could be uploaded by users for prediction within the application.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy (97%) as the primary performance metric without statistical inference.

HAM10000.notes: The model was trained for 20 epochs using 80% of the dataset for training and 20% for testing. Data augmentation and RandomOverSampler were applied to handle class imbalance. The system also provided detailed medical information and hospital recommendations for predicted diseases.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with DenseNet201 architecture
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using DenseNet201 (pretrained on ImageNet)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hierarchical feature extraction via transfer learning

preprocessing_techniques: Removing missing values (ensuring no null values in the dataset)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the HAM10000 dataset to artificially increase the size and diversity of the dataset by applying various transformations to existing data. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation angles, flip probabilities) were not reported in the text.

Used to address class imbalance by resampling data, specifically by randomly selecting samples from the minority class and replacing them with new ones in the training dataset. This is a sampling-based augmentation technique for class balancing.

conclusions: The proposed CNN framework using DenseNet201 transfer learning achieved a classification accuracy of 97% for diagnosing seven distinct skin disease categories on the HAM10000 dataset.

The system functions as a decision support tool that provides detailed medical information (overview, symptoms, risk factors, and prevention) for predicted diseases and recommends nearby healthcare facilities.

The study highlights the potential of deep learning-based diagnostic tools to support dermatologists, enable early detection, and facilitate remote healthcare solutions, particularly in resource-limited or rural environments.
The authors acknowledge that physicians require human judgment and intuition and cannot be replaced by automated systems.

The study notes that challenges remain in ensuring consistent accuracy across diverse skin tones, imaging conditions, and disease severity levels, and that integration of explainable AI and real-world clinical validation is an area for ongoing research.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset for both training and testing, with no external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting generalizability.

The paper acknowledges that the model's accuracy may vary across diverse skin tones, imaging conditions, and disease severity levels, indicating potential bias and lack of robustness in real-world settings.
The system is presented as a decision support tool rather than a diagnostic replacement, explicitly stating that physicians' human judgment and intuition cannot be replaced by automated systems.
The paper does not report detailed per-class performance metrics (e.g., precision, recall, F1-score) or statistical significance tests, relying solely on overall accuracy (97%).

The application allows users to upload 'any type of skin image' for prediction, raising concerns about data leakage if test images from HAM10000 are used, and potential deployment risks with non-dermoscopic images.
The study addresses class imbalance using RandomOverSampler, which may lead to overfitting on minority classes without addressing the underlying distribution shift in real-world populations.
The paper lacks discussion on explainable AI techniques or real-world clinical validation, which are noted as important areas for ongoing research.
